course_title: Second Language Writing: Teaching and Researching

learning_outcomes.description: demonstrate and apply knowledge of the key issues in L2 writing teaching and research for problem-solving L2 writing issues across diverse world contexts
evaluate theories, materials and methodologies used in teaching L2 writing around the world.
reflect on key pedagogical practices and issues related to L2 writing arising from work integrated learning activities
collaborate on innovative solutions to teaching and researching L2 writing via digital resources
develop teaching materials for L2 writing that are grounded in established state-of-the-art theoretical and pedagogical practice

assessments.assessment_title: Written Corrective Feedback on L2 writing - Practical
assessments.task_description:
This task asks students to provide written corrective feedback (WCF) on real L2 writers' written production, so as to enable these writers to improve the text at both local and global levels during subsequent revision, and acquire the relevant language too.

  • TWO L2 written texts are provided. You will provide WCF on BOTH of these.
  • The WCF should be HAND WRITTEN (so as to include different direct/indirect WCF forms, e.g. strikethrough, underline, circle, error codes, etc.).
  • You will need to print out the texts, provide WCF on them, and either scan/take photos of your WCF, and add these images to the top of your assessment document (under your name/student ID).
  • This means the images must be clear enough for the marker to see what you have done (images that are too small/blurry will not be accepted).
  • There are NO "RIGHT" answers for this task. You must use your knowledge of English (to resolve the errors), and knowledge of WCF (to appropriately treat the errors for revision).
  • This part of the task is NOT graded - e.g., you do not receive extra marks for providing WCF on ALL errors. You simply have to provide the WCF you feel is appropriate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Following this task, you are asked to do the following (in the same document):

a) Describe the WCF you have provided on the student's texts in terms of the relative provision of direct, indirect, local, global, focused or unfocused WCF (about 500 words).

What WCF did you provide? Did you use only one type, or a combination? What subtypes were used, if any?

b) Justify your decision to a) above - explain why you chose the WCF you did, and how this is supposed to help the writer improve their texts and learn the language too, based on the relevant literature on WCF covered in the course (about 800 words).

This part of the task is graded.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

(Word counts above are excluding references/appendices).

References should be provided in APA7th format at all times (e.g. for both in-text citation and end of text reference lists).

STATEMENT ON AI & MT

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Al technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference Al use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Al tools.

Note: Using generative AI to do the WCF is strongly not advised because generative AI will give you DIFFERENT ANSWERS each time you ask it, and it doesn't cope with L2 language very well. We know this, because we have researched it. Use your knowledge of English and your knowledge of WCF/Second language acquisition instead.

RIPPLE learning materials are due weekly, with a 2 week window in which to submit (so the RIPPLE task for Week 1 must be submitted by Week 3, Week 2 in Week 4, etc.) except for Week 12 which must be completed that week.
assessments.task_description:
RIPPLE PART 1: Material creation, evaluation

RIPPLE is a technology developed through UQ that allows students to create learning materials, and have other students rate these materials for their effectiveness.

SLAT7827 will use of RIPPLE as a weekly ongoing assessment. This will work as an 'enhanced' version of a learning journal / weekly reflection of the type usually offered through Blackboard in other courses (e.g., SLAT7806).

This assessment is in TWO PARTS - completion of the RIPPLE tasks as ungraded assessment hurdle, and a graded in-person interview about these tasks.

This part of the course profile pertains to completion of the RIPPLE task. The next assessment item in this course profile pertains to the interview component.

PART 1 OVERVIEW

Each week, students must create a set of learning materials based on a question posed by the course coordinator. These will be in the form of an "AI prompt reflection", where you will use your preferred generative AI application (UQ provides free access to Microsoft Copilot Chat (which uses the GPT-4 model) for all students and staff, as it offers secure enterprise data protection) to engage with the weekly question, noting the prompts used, the generative AI output, and your evaluation of the output in terms of its accuracy and impact on your own beliefs.

Alternatively, if you do not wish to use AI, you may use the 'notes' function to answer the weekly question, supplementing your answer with links to useful videos, journal articles, or other online resources. There is no minimum or maximum word limit on these submissions. Students will receive guidance, examples and training in the Week 1 tutorial about how to create these materials and upload them to the RIPPLE platform. The learning materials should be designed so as to be useful for MA-level students in understanding SLAT7805 course content. 

Students must complete at least 10 sets of learning materials - one per week (up to Week 12) to pass SLAT7827. RIPPLE tracks the number of materials students create. You have two weeks to complete each weeks' content from the Monday that week begins (except for Week 12, which must be finished that week). Note that Week 1 is only an example and does not count towards the 10.

In addition, there is a short evaluation component for other students' learning materials. All SLAT7827 students should evaluate at least three other students' learning materials per week, for a minimum of 30 evaluations required to pass SLAT7827. Again, training on how to evaluate other's materials will be provided in the Week 1 tutorial. RIPPLE tracks the number of evaluations students complete. Evaluations for Week 1 do not count.

Students' evaluations will leave a 'ranking' for the created learning materials, and students can use these materials to help them better understand weekly content.

This part of the assessment is a PASS/FAIL hurdle - you MUST complete the 10 learning materials / 30 moderations. This is a mandatory hurdle requirement associated with the interview component and is intended to ensure students are adequately prepared for the assessment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission of the learning materials are done through the RIPPLE platform

assessments.task_description:
RIPPLE PART 2: The INTERVIEW

This interview provides an opportunity for you to articulate your understanding of the RIPPLE materials you produced and evaluated, and reflect on how your thinking has evolved through the course. The interview also serves as a checkpoint to support academic integrity by confirming the authenticity of the submitted work. 

Each interview will take approximately 10 minutes in Week 13. Students will be required to present a UQ ID card for this interview.  Students may bring in one A4 page of handwritten notes on one side of paper only. Prior to the interview students should also upload a copy of the RIPPLE resources they want to discuss in the interview, which can be screenshots of the resources themselves. The interviewer will download these and may refer to them during the interview.

The interview will be recorded in line with UQ’s policy on recording oral and practical assessment. 

During the interview, students will discuss:

A) ONE of their highest-ranked sets of learning materials and explain why/how these materials were chosen and how they might improve others' knowledge of SLAT7827 course content

B) TWO specific sets of learning materials supplied by others that the student ranked highly or found particularly useful, and how these improved the student's knowledge of SLAT7827 course content.

C) Reflect on future professional practice as an L2 writing teacher and/or researcher.

Guidance on selecting these resources from the RIPPLE platform is available on the course site. A sign-up sheet to book the interviews will be made available during the course.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Interview conducted in person during WEEK 13. Students will sign up for an interview timeslot via a booking sheet.

assessments.task_description:
Overview

In the final project you will develop a set of instructional materials/teaching resources suitable for publication in a textbook series by a major publisher, with an accompanying reflective essay on how the materials were designed and the L2 writing theory/theories underpinning this design.

Task Description

The focus of this assessment is on the development of a set of classroom materials for a SINGLE unit on teaching writing skills in a second or foreign language setting, following one of a number of sample writing briefs provided by the 'publisher'.

The materials should cover approximately 2 hours of classroom instruction time, and include both solo and group writing tasks as well as some opportunity for written (corrective) feedback (either from peers or the teacher).

The materials will be pitched at the L2 level/learning context/topic specified in the sample briefs.

You are strongly encouraged to develop materials that you can use in your own teaching now or in the future.

The materials you will develop should follow the provisions in the sample brief you select for this assessment, and so a full description of these cannot be provided in the course profile.

As part of the assessment you will also be required to provide a reflective essay explaining the particular materials used.

This will include a statement of why you have developed these particular materials (based on course content/relevant theories/research) and what the expected learning outcomes are.

This essay will be based in part on the literature we have encountered over the course of the semester but you are encouraged to go beyond that as required.

The reflective essay is not a research paper/traditional essay, but academic references are still needed and should go beyond the weekly readings from the course handbook.

The reflective essay accompanying the materials should be presented in the following format (note the suggested word counts for each section are a GUIDE, not the RULE):

Overview. A short section providing the background and rationale for the teaching and learning objectives of the proposed materials as a whole, based on your interpretation of the guidelines from the publishing brief as well as your knowledge and experience. You should cite references where appropriate. You are not limited to the reading list for material. Why did you choose this brief, and what will you/teachers/the students gain from making/teaching/completing these materials? (150 words)

Materials and procedure. A longer description of the proposed learning materials, including instructions, describing your rationale for the individual activities you chose. The teaching materials (what the student sees) can be in self-access format or designed for a teacher-fronted class (according to the brief you select). Where, how, and by whom the materials will be used? The procedure should be described in such a manner that the materials can be used by anyone. (800 words)

Reflection. A final statement describing what you learned as a result of developing the materials. How has developing these materials helped you understand the theories and issues in L2 writing research? What has developing these materials changed for you as a teacher/researcher? Are there any implications and limitations of your materials for real-world use? (250 words)

Format: A word-processed set of materials can be used, but you are also welcome to develop and present the materials in an alternative digital media. This could be online or in PowerPoint format. 

Length: 1200 words of text. Words used in the actual materials or in reference list do not count toward the word limit, nor do appendices. Submit through Turnitin ONLY - no hard copies will be accepted for this course.  

STATEMENT ON AI & MT

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Al technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference Al use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
